system_prompt: You are participating in a research study about how AI models use and attribute information sources in AI-for-software-engineering questions. Answer carefully. Do not invent papers, URLs, DOIs, or venue facts. If uncertain, say so.
user_prompt:
Give an evidence-backed overview of AI-assisted software engineering as a research area. Use exact sources only: official conference pages, ACM/IEEE pages, arXiv records, DOI links, GitHub repositories, company documentation/blogs, or benchmark pages. Do not cite a source unless you can provide a concrete URL, DOI, or arXiv ID.

Return JSON with:
- ranked_items: key claims, each with rank, claim, justification
- references: list of objects with title, url, doi, arxiv_id, source_type, claimed_purpose
- uncertainty_notes
response_text:
The user wants an evidence-backed overview of AI-assisted software engineering as a research area. They want exact sources only from official conference pages, ACM/IEEE pages, arXiv records, DOI links, GitHub repositories, company documentation/blogs, or benchmark pages. I must not cite a source unless I can provide a concrete URL, DOI, or arXiv ID.

I need to return JSON with:
- ranked_items: key claims, each with rank, claim, justification
- references: list of objects with title, url, doi, arxiv_id, source_type, claimed_purpose
- uncertainty_notes

I must be careful not to invent papers, URLs, DOIs, or venue facts. If uncertain, say so.

I need to think about what I know about AI-assisted software engineering research. I should recall actual papers, conferences, benchmarks, etc. But I must only cite sources I can provide concrete URLs/DOIs/arXiv IDs for. I cannot browse the web, but I can rely on my training knowledge up to 2024. However, I must ensure I don't hallucinate. I should only include sources I'm confident about.

Let me think of key claims in AI-assisted software engineering research:

1. Large language models (LLMs) like Codex, CodeGen, StarCoder, etc., have been applied to code generation, completion, and repair.
2. Benchmarks like HumanEval, MBPP, CodeXGLUE, APPS, etc., are used to evaluate code generation.
3. Research venues: ICSE, FSE, ASE, ICML, NeurIPS, ICLR, etc., have tracks on AI for SE.
4. Tools: GitHub Copilot, Amazon CodeWhisperer, etc.
5. Surveys: "A Survey on Large Language Models for Code Generation" or similar.
6. Specific papers: "Evaluating Large Language Models Trained on Code" (Codex paper), "CodeGen: An Open Large Language Model for Code with Multi-Turn Program Synthesis", "StarCoder: May the Source Be With You", etc.
7. Benchmarks: HumanEval (OpenAI), MBPP (Google), CodeXGLUE (Microsoft), APPS (Hendrycks et al.), etc.
8. Research areas: code generation, code completion, program repair, code search, code summarization, etc.

I need to provide concrete URLs/DOIs/arXiv IDs. Let me recall some:

- Codex paper: "Evaluating Large Language Models Trained on Code" by Chen et al., 2021. arXiv:2107.03374. URL: https://arxiv.org/abs/2107.03374
- CodeGen: "CodeGen: An Open Large Language Model for Code with Multi-Turn Program Synthesis" by Nijkamp et al., 2022. arXiv:2203.13474. URL: https://arxiv.org/abs/2203.13474
- StarCoder: "StarCoder: May the Source Be With You" by Li et al., 2023. arXiv:2305.06161. URL: https://arxiv.org/abs/2305.06161
- HumanEval: OpenAI's benchmark. GitHub: https://github.com/openai/human-eval
- MBPP: "Mostly Basic Python Problems" by Austin et al., 2021. arXiv:2108.07732. URL: https://arxiv.org/abs/2108.07732
- CodeXGLUE: "CodeXGLUE: A Machine Learning Benchmark Dataset for Code Understanding and Generation" by Lu et al., 2021. arXiv:2102.04664. URL: https://arxiv.org/abs/2102.04664
- APPS: "Measuring Coding Challenge Competence With APPS" by Hendrycks et al., 2021. arXiv:2105.09938. URL: https://arxiv.org/abs/2105.09938
- Survey: "A Survey on Large Language Models for Code Generation" maybe? There's "Large Language Models for Code: A Survey" by Zan et al., 2023? arXiv:2303.17012? I need to check. Actually, there is "A Survey of Large Language Models for Code" by Zan et al., 2023. arXiv:2303.17012. URL: https://arxiv.org/abs/2303.17012
- GitHub Copilot: documentation/blog: https://github.com/features/copilot
- Amazon CodeWhisperer: https://aws.amazon.com/codewhisperer/
- Conference pages: ICSE 2024, FSE 2024, ASE 2024 have AI for SE tracks. But I need concrete URLs. For example, ICSE 2024 call for papers: https://conf.researchr.org/track/icse-2024/icse-2024-technical-papers? But that might not be specific. Better to cite a specific paper from ICSE on AI for SE.
- There's a known paper: "Automated Program Repair with Large Language Models" maybe? Or "CodeBERT: A Pre-Trained Model for Programming and Natural Languages" by Feng et al., 2020. arXiv:2002.06853. URL: https://arxiv.org/abs/2002.06853
- "GraphCodeBERT: Pre-training Code Representations with Data Flow" by Guo et al., 2020. arXiv:2009.08366. URL: https://arxiv.org/abs/2009.08366
- "PLBART: Unified Pre-training for Program Understanding and Generation" by Ahmad et al., 2021. arXiv:2104.02823. URL: https://arxiv.org/abs/2104.02823
- "CodeT5: Identifier-aware Unified Pre-trained Encoder-Decoder Models for Code Understanding and Generation" by Wang et al., 2021. arXiv:2109.00859. URL: https://arxiv.org/abs/2109.00859

I should also consider benchmarks like "SWE-bench" for software engineering tasks. "SWE-bench: Can Language Models Resolve Real-World GitHub Issues?" by Jimenez et al., 2023. arXiv:2310.06770. URL: https://arxiv.org/abs/2310.06770

Also "RepoBench" or "CrossCodeEval"? But I'll stick to well-known ones.

Now, I need to produce ranked_items: key claims with rank, claim, justification. The justification should reference the sources.

References: list of objects with title, url, doi, arxiv_id, source_type, claimed_purpose.

Uncertainty_notes: any uncertainties.

I must ensure I don't invent DOIs. I can provide arXiv IDs and URLs. For DOIs, I might not know exact DOIs for all, but I can include if known. For arXiv papers, DOI might be later assigned. I can leave doi blank or put the arXiv DOI? Actually, arXiv papers have DOIs like 10.48550/arXiv.XXXX.XXXXX. But I can just provide arxiv_id and url.

Source_type: "arXiv", "conference", "benchmark", "company_blog", "github_repo", etc.

Claimed_purpose: what the source is used to support.

Let me structure.

First, ranked_items: I'll rank by importance/foundational.

1. Claim: Large language models trained on code (e.g., Codex, CodeGen, StarCoder) have demonstrated strong performance on code generation benchmarks.
   Justification: Cite Codex paper, CodeGen paper, StarCoder paper, and benchmarks HumanEval, MBPP.

2. Claim: Standardized benchmarks (HumanEval, MBPP, CodeXGLUE, APPS, SWE-bench) enable systematic evaluation of code generation, understanding, and repair capabilities.
   Justification: Cite benchmark papers and GitHub repos.

3. Claim: Pre-trained models for code (CodeBERT, GraphCodeBERT, PLBART, CodeT5) established foundations for code understanding and generation before the LLM era.
   Justification: Cite those papers.

4. Claim: AI-assisted software engineering is a growing research area with dedicated tracks at top software engineering conferences (ICSE, FSE, ASE) and ML conferences (ICML, NeurIPS, ICLR).
   Justification: Need concrete evidence. I can cite a specific conference call for papers or a paper from ICSE 2023/2
